column1: # Power BI Dashboard — Structure & Build Guide

This document describes the dashboard to build in Power BI Desktop using `data/supplier_quality_data.csv` and the DAX measures in `docs/dax_measures.md`. Once built, export a screenshot (or the `.pbix`, if file size allows) and drop it in this folder.
**Purpose:** answer "how risky is our supplier base, right now?" in one glance.

column3: Total Suppliers, Avg Composite Risk Score, # Tier 4 (High Risk), # Tier 3 (Elevated Risk)
Avg Composite Risk Score by Category (sorted ascending — worst first)
Avg Composite Risk Score by Country
Top 10 highest-risk suppliers: name, country, category, composite score, risk tier — conditionally formatted (red/amber/green)

column1: **Purpose:** let a user investigate a single supplier's risk drivers.

column3: Composite Risk Score for selected supplier, with tier band as target
Breakdown of the 5 component scores (Defect, Complaint, Delivery, Audit, Financial) for selected supplier
Trend of complaints / defects over time (if extending dataset with monthly granularity)
Raw underlying metrics for the selected supplier

column1: **Purpose:** demonstrate how weight assumptions change risk classification — a strong talking point in interviews.

column3: One per risk driver weight (must sum to 100% — add a validation card)
Suppliers whose risk tier changes as weights are adjusted

column1: - Use a consistent 3-color risk palette across every page: green (Tier 1–2), amber (Tier 3), red (Tier 4) — this is what makes the dashboard scannable in 10 seconds, which is the standard a real risk-monitoring tool is held to.
- Keep KPI cards at the top of every page — executives look there first.
- Add a tooltip page explaining the composite score formula, so the dashboard is self-documenting for anyone reviewing it without the README.
Once built, add exported screenshots here as `page1_overview.png`, `page2_drilldown.png`, etc., and reference them in the main `README.md`.
